QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to development
projects of the following Architectural Resources Collaborative, Inc. ("ARC") clients:
Triad Downtown Vallejo, LLC; Empress Theatre Associates, LLC; Triad 236 Georgra
Street, LLC;Triad Communities, LP, Triad Development, Inc.?
2. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to Lennar Mare
lsland, LLC's ("Lennar") development of the Mare Island upland parcel ("EETP") or
other Lennar development projects?
3. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to Touro
University's ("Touro") development of the Campus Parcel and Reuse Area 1A?
4. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to the approval of
fees, capital improvement projects or the annual budget and budget amendments for the
East Course and West Course?
5. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions involving Mr. Buck
Kamphausen?
CONCLUSIONS
1-3. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in governmental decisions that
come before the city council or the city's redevelopment agency involving ARC clients if
there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on ARC, which is a source of
income to him. Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in
making, or influence governmental decisions where ARC is named the architect of record
on these clients' projects, or in other decisions that result in ARC's annual gross revenues
increasing or decreasing by $20,000 or more.
Additionally, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in making, or
influence any decision in which the nents rule applies (as discussed below). Specifically,
he must consider whether governmental decisions before the city council or
redevelopment agency will achieve a goal or purpose, which will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on ARC or any source of income to him, which he is paid to
bring about. If a nexus is found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on ARC,
even a penny's worth, is deemed material.
4. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in these decisions if there is a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his personal finances. Specifically, he
may not participate if any of these decisions result in his personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities (or those of his immediate family) increasing or decreasing by at least
$250 in a 12-month period.

5. Mr. Kamphausen, through his control of the Neptune Trust, is a source of
income to Councilmember Wilson. Therefore, Councilmember Wilson may not
participate in decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon Mr. Kamphausen. Specifically, Councilmember Wilson may not participate in
decisions that affect Mr. Kamphallsen's income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or more.
FACTS
You seek advice on behalf of Michael Wilson, a member of the Vallejo City
Council and the city's redevelopment agency ("Agency"). You wish to know whether
Councilmember Wilson may participate in decisions related to a number of development
projects and other matters. Councilmember Wilson assumed the office of councilmember
and Agency board member on December 4,2007.
Architectural Resources Collaborative. Inc. ("ARC")
Councilmember Wilson is employed as the Chief Financial Officer and Vice
President of ARC. Mr. Wilson is not an architect and does not provide architectural
services on any of the projects. He is a salaried employee and receives no commissions
or bonuses based on the revenues derived from any clients, projects or agreements,
though the value of his investment in the company (held in a family tmst) may increase if
the gross revenues of ARC were to increase.
Councilmember Wilson has a five percent (57o\ ownership interest in ARC. The
natllre of his investment is stock and its fair market value is between $100,001 and
$1,000,000.00. His share of ARC Inc. is held by the Peter L. Wilson and Michael L.
Wilson Family Trust and he is a co-trustee of the trust. ARC has provided architectural
services to a number of developers and businesses in the City of Vallejo.
You state in your March 18, 2008 letter that ARC will not be initiating any
proceedings, be a named party in, or the subject of any development projects before the
city council or the city's redevelopment agency. However, ARC may be listed as the
architect of record for some development projects initiated by its clients (listed below).
You estimate that on the projects where ARC is the architect of record, ARC's annual
gross revenues will be increased or decreased by $20,000 or more.
Clients of "ARC"
Triad DeveloDment, Inc. crntl Related Entities:

working through its affiliate, Triatl Communities, LP, and is involved in a number of

council and the Agency's board for approval.

In your March 18, 2008 letter, you state that ARC is not the exclusive architect of

However, the City became a client of ARC for its work on the East Course before

Under the Lease and the Management Agreement between the City and the Club,

The Club has a number of types of memberships. Prior to assuming office,

basis to enter into the approximately 22 toumaments sponsored by the Club annually

("Wilson Familv Trust")
Councilmember Wilson and Peter Wilson are trustees of the Wilson Family Trust.
Neither receives a salary for serving as a tmstee. Councilmember Wilson has an interest
in the Wilson Family trust purslrant to Regulation 18234(c).
Ownership interest of 5 oercent in ARC Inc.
The Wilson Family Tmst holds the Councilmember's share of ARC stock valued
between $100,001 and $1,000,000.
Wilson Enterprise, LLC ("Wilson Enterprise" ) and the Florida Street Property
The Wilson Family Tnrst holds Councilmember Wilson's and his domestic
partner's share of Wilson Enterprise, LLC ("Wilson Enterprise"); Each own twenty-five

ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
goverxmental decision. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-(8).)
Steps I & 2: Is Councilmember Wilson a "public official" and will he be
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. . . ." (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a)(l).) As an elected

Page No. 8
member of the city council, Councilmember Wilson is a public official subject to the
Act's conflict of interest provisions.s
o
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any coLlrse of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See Regulation 18702.1.)
Councilmember Wilson will "make a governmental decision" if he votes on any
issue obligating the city. In addition to actually voting on these matters, if he engages in
negotiations without significant substantive review, provides advice or make
recommendations, he will be "participating" in a decision (Regulati on Ig70Z.2). He will
also be "influencing" that decision if he appears before or otherwise attempt to influence
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Step 3: What are Councilmember Wilson,s economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 103(d); Regulation 18703. t(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation L8703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);

In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finonces, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal
financial effects" mle (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
Sources of Income:
Architectural Resources Collcrborotive, Inc. ( "ARC" )
A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) An official also has an
economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 103(d); Regulation 1 8703. 1(b)).
For purposes of this analysis we assume that Councilmember Wilson has earned
income of $500 or more from ARC in the past 12 months. He also has a 5 percent
ownership interest in ARC stock through the Wilson Family Tmst worth between
$100,001 to $1,000,000. In addition, he is an employee of ARC and in a management
position within the company.
Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson has an economic interest in ARC: (1) as a
source of income; (2) as an employee and because of his management position in the
firm; and (3) because of his investment of $2,000 or more in the company. (Section
87103(a),(c), and (d).)
However, because he has less than I0 percent ownershipa interest in the company,
the clients of ARC are not sources of income to Councilmember Wilson.) (Section
82030(a).)
' For purposes of this analysis, we assume that Councilmember Wilson's domestic partner does
not have an ownership interest in ARC of 5 percent or greater. If the councilmember's domestic partner
has 5 percent or greater interest in ARC, the councilmember (who himself has a 5 percent interest in ARC)

(Section 82030; Regulation 18229.)

Trust cutd Mr,
From the facts you have provided, Councilmember Wilson has received a
personal loan from the Neptune Trust, a business trust controlled by Mr. Kamphausen.
The Neptune Trust made a $465,000 loan to the four members of Wilson Enterprise, LLC
as individuals, including Councilmember Wilson.
The term "income" as nsed in the Act includes loans. (Section 82030.)
Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson has an economic interest in Neptune Trust as a
soLlrce of income. Whether Mr. KamphaLlsen, who controls Neptune Trust, is also a
source of income to Councilmember Wilson depends on whether, under these facts, it is
appropriate to "pierce" through the organizational structllre of the trust.
Business trusts are business entities under the Act. (Section 82005; Regulation
18705.1(c)(a).) For conflict of interest purposes, the Commission has "pierced" through
business entities, when ownership or control of the entity is vested in certain persons. (In
re Lumsdon (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140 [closely held corporation pierced to reach majority
shareholder for reporting purposes'1.) When this piercing occurs, the economic interest is
deemed to exist in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (ln
re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6 flimited partner has an economic interest in each
controlling general partnerl; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. 4-80-069 [official who is
employed by closely held corporation also has economic interest in its president/majority
shareholderl; Lahr Advice Letter, No. I-98-298 [public official who has an economic
interest in a corporation also has an economic interest in the corporation's sole
shareholder.l.)
Your facts indicate that Mr. Kamphausen has a controlling interest in the Neptune
Society of Central California, a for-profit business entity. He also "controls" Neptune
Trust, and income from the trust is provided to beneficiaries of the trust, which are
corporations wholly owned by Mr. Kamphausen. [n addition, Mr. Kamphausen, as
trLlstee, invests the Trust funds and charges a fee for his services.
In light of Mr. Kamphausen's status as the individual who controls the Neptune
Trust, who receives income from the trust, and whose wholly-owned corporations are
beneficiaries of the trust, it is appropriate to "pierce" through the trust entity for purposes
of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act and attribute income received from the
Neptune Trust as income received from Mr. Kamphausen.
Thus, we would consider both the Neptune Trust and Mr. Kamphausen as
economic interests of Councilmember Wilson.

Real Property
Florida Street P roperty:
Councilmember Wilson and his domestic partner each own twenty-five percent of
Wilson Enterprise, LLC, which is held by the Wilson Family Trust. Wilson Enterprise,

Family Trust pursuant to terms of Regulation 18234(c). Therefore, the assets of the

(Talley Advice Letter, No. 4-96-204; Silver Advice Letter, No. 4-96-066; Irmas Advice

Although you included the Florida Street Property in your facts, you do not seek

Personal Finances:
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103; regularion 18703.5.) A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing. (lbid.)
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Wilson's personal
finances as the result of the proposed development agreements and other governmental
decisions. But note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have an effect on Councilmember Wilson's personal finances.
Step 4: Are councilmember wilson's economic interests directry or
indirectly involved in the decisions?
Sources of Income:
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a person, including business entities and sources
of income to a public official, is directly involved in a decision before the official's

agency when the source of income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar reqllest or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the subject person."
For purposes of determining the materiality standard to be applied under Step 5,
sollrces of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the
rules in Regulation 18704.1(b) are deemed indirectly involved under Regulation 18705.3

Therefore, based on the facts presented, ARC is not directly involved in the

For indirectly involved persons, including business entities, the materiality

would be initiating, or be the subject of, or a named party in, any decision before the city
council or the Agency." Therefore. they are deemed indirectly involved.

Personal Finances:
It is presumed that Councilmember Wilson has an ecbnomic interest in his
personal finances and those of his immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. If there is any financial effect on an official's
personal finances, they are deemed to be directly involved in a decision. Therefore, if
there is an interest, it is directly affected by the decision.
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Wilson's personal
finances as the result of upcoming decisions, but note that a conflict of interest may arise
if it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on his personal
finances.
For instance if the city council decisions to approve green fees, tournament
member fees, and other fees involving the Vallejo Golf Club, lnc. will impact
Councilmember Wilson's personal finances or those of his immediate family either
positively or negatively (such as increasing or reducing the amount he pays in
membership or tournament fees), his personal finances are deemed to be directly
involved in a decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps 5 & 6: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Wilson's economic interest(s)?
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is first necessary to
identify the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standards will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (ln re Thorner, sllpra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (1n
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.

Page No. 14

Business EntiN:
For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of less than
$500,000 for the most recent fiscal year) the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(cXa) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( 1)-(3),
the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $ 20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more;
of,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of g 20,000 or
more."
Thus, if the economic impact of the decisions regarding ARC and its clients or the
Neptune Trust meets the above thresholds, the impact will be material.
According to your facts, governmental decisions where ARC is named the
architect of record by one of its clients will result in ARC's annual gross revenues
increasing or decreasing by $20,000 or more. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that
the materiality standard will be met as the result of those decisions. Accordingly,
Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in making, or influence any decision
in which ARC is named by one of its clients as the architect of record on a project, or on
any project that will have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on ARC.
Sources qf Income Who Are Individuals:
Under Regulation 18705.3, an effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a sollrce of income to an official if any of the following applies:


File No. I-08-017
Page No. 15
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."
The above materiality standard is applicable to Mr. Kamphausen, who is a source
of income to Councilmember Wilson.
You have not included any information regarding possible financial impacts of
the decisions on Mr. Kamphausen. However, if any decision meets the above thresholds,
the impact will be material. Therefore, Councilmember Wilson may not participate in
those decisions involving Mr. Kamphausen that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect upon Mr. Kamphausen.
Nents Rule
In addition, there is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in
cases where there is a "nexus" between duties an official owes to a source of income and
to the official's own public agency. The materiality threshold is understandably much
lower when a public official is paid to accomplish some action that is within the official's
public decision-making authority. Under the nexus rule, "Any reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed
material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
(Regulation I 8705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official,
we presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in
situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and
control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice
Letter, No. A-03-1471' Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.)
You state in your letter that Councilmember Wilson is the Chief Financial Officer
and Vice President of ARC. A nexus may exist, and a material financial effect may
occLlr, if a particular governmental decision in which a high-level administrator like
Councilmember Wilson is asked to participate would also achieve, defeat, aid or hinder a
policy, plrrpose, or goal of ARC. (see Moser, supra; Ronkin Advice Letter, No. A-94-
310.)
You have not presented enough facts for us to analyze fully whether the nexus test

Page No. 16
is met. Councilmember Wilson must consider whether the various govemmental
decisions before the city cor.rncil will achieve a goal or purpose that he is paid to bring
about. If a nexus is found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on ARC from a
governmental decision relating to various development projects, even a penny's worth, is
deemed material.
Personal Financial Effects :
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in regulation
18705.5, which provides in pertinent part:
"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public _official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period."7 lRegulation 18705.5)
Thus, if the economic impact of the decisions on Councilmember Wilson's
personal finances meets the above threshold, the impact will be material.
You have not provided any information regarding potential effects of these
decisions on councilmember wilson's finances. Therefore, we cannot make a
determination regarding this issue. However, if the decisions will increase or decrease
Councilmember Wilson's personal expenses (such as Club membership fees or other
Club related expenses), income, assets, or liabilities or that of his or her immediate family
by $250 in any 12-month period, this materiality standard will be met as the result of
these decisions.
steps 7 & 8: Public Generally and Legally Required participation
Exceptions
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 1 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called Llpon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict
of interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies
only in certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be
paralyzed from acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the oflicial. nor a flnancial etfbct on the

direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered.

You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the

circumstances, so we will not address them further.

sections.conclusion:
1-3. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in governmental decisions that
come before the city council or the city's redevelopment agency involving ARC clients if
there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on ARC, which is a source of
income to him. Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in
making, or influence governmental decisions where ARC is named the architect of record
on these clients' projects, or in other decisions that result in ARC's annual gross revenues
increasing or decreasing by $20,000 or more.
Additionally, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in making, or
influence any decision in which the nents rule applies (as discussed below). Specifically,
he must consider whether governmental decisions before the city council or
redevelopment agency will achieve a goal or purpose, which will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on ARC or any source of income to him, which he is paid to
bring about. If a nexus is found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on ARC,
even a penny's worth, is deemed material.
4. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in these decisions if there is a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his personal finances. Specifically, he
may not participate if any of these decisions result in his personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities (or those of his immediate family) increasing or decreasing by at least
$250 in a 12-month period.
5. Mr. Kamphausen, through his control of the Neptune Trust, is a source of
income to Councilmember Wilson. Therefore, Councilmember Wilson may not
participate in decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon Mr. Kamphausen. Specifically, Councilmember Wilson may not participate in
decisions that affect Mr. Kamphallsen's income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or more.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
goverxmental decision. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-(8).)
Steps I & 2: Is Councilmember Wilson a "public official" and will he be
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. . . ." (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a)(l).) As an elected
member of the city council, Councilmember Wilson is a public official subject to the
Act's conflict of interest provisions.s
o
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any coLlrse of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See Regulation 18702.1.)
Councilmember Wilson will "make a governmental decision" if he votes on any
issue obligating the city. In addition to actually voting on these matters, if he engages in
negotiations without significant substantive review, provides advice or make
recommendations, he will be "participating" in a decision (Regulati on Ig70Z.2). He will
also be "influencing" that decision if he appears before or otherwise attempt to influence
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Step 3: What are Councilmember Wilson,s economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 103(d); Regulation 18703. t(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation L8703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);

A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) An official also has an
economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 103(d); Regulation 1 8703. 1(b)).
For purposes of this analysis we assume that Councilmember Wilson has earned
income of $500 or more from ARC in the past 12 months. He also has a 5 percent
ownership interest in ARC stock through the Wilson Family Tmst worth between
$100,001 to $1,000,000. In addition, he is an employee of ARC and in a management
position within the company.
Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson has an economic interest in ARC: (1) as a
source of income; (2) as an employee and because of his management position in the
firm; and (3) because of his investment of $2,000 or more in the company. (Section
87103(a),(c), and (d).)
However, because he has less than I0 percent ownershipa interest in the company,
the clients of ARC are not sources of income to Councilmember Wilson.) (Section
82030(a).)
' For purposes of this analysis, we assume that Councilmember Wilson's domestic partner does
not have an ownership interest in ARC of 5 percent or greater. If the councilmember's domestic partner
has 5 percent or greater interest in ARC, the councilmember (who himself has a 5 percent interest in ARC)
will be deemed to have a l0 percent or greater interest in the company. If so, Councilmember Wilson will
have an economic interest in every client of the business that pays the firm a sutficient amount such that his
pro-rata share of the income which represents his community property interest equals or exceeds $500.
(Section 82030; Regulation 18229.)
) We note that you have also asked whether Councilmember Wilson may participate in decisions
related to development projects or other business activities in which any of the managing members of the
above clients of ARC may be personally involved, or have a contmlling interest in. a business entity
appearing befbre the City Council or Agency Board. Because this question does not involve any particular
governmental decision and you do not provide any tacts. we are unable to analyze this question. However.
if in the future issues arise tbr you regarding a specific decision. you may submit additional facts and
request further advice.
Trust cutd Mr,
From the facts you have provided, Councilmember Wilson has received a
personal loan from the Neptune Trust, a business trust controlled by Mr. Kamphausen.
The Neptune Trust made a $465,000 loan to the four members of Wilson Enterprise, LLC
as individuals, including Councilmember Wilson.
The term "income" as nsed in the Act includes loans. (Section 82030.)
Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson has an economic interest in Neptune Trust as a
soLlrce of income. Whether Mr. KamphaLlsen, who controls Neptune Trust, is also a
source of income to Councilmember Wilson depends on whether, under these facts, it is
appropriate to "pierce" through the organizational structllre of the trust.
Business trusts are business entities under the Act. (Section 82005; Regulation
18705.1(c)(a).) For conflict of interest purposes, the Commission has "pierced" through
business entities, when ownership or control of the entity is vested in certain persons. (In
re Lumsdon (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140 [closely held corporation pierced to reach majority
shareholder for reporting purposes'1.) When this piercing occurs, the economic interest is
deemed to exist in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (ln
re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6 flimited partner has an economic interest in each
controlling general partnerl; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. 4-80-069 [official who is
employed by closely held corporation also has economic interest in its president/majority
shareholderl; Lahr Advice Letter, No. I-98-298 [public official who has an economic
interest in a corporation also has an economic interest in the corporation's sole
shareholder.l.)
Your facts indicate that Mr. Kamphausen has a controlling interest in the Neptune
Society of Central California, a for-profit business entity. He also "controls" Neptune
Trust, and income from the trust is provided to beneficiaries of the trust, which are
corporations wholly owned by Mr. Kamphausen. [n addition, Mr. Kamphausen, as
trLlstee, invests the Trust funds and charges a fee for his services.
In light of Mr. Kamphausen's status as the individual who controls the Neptune
Trust, who receives income from the trust, and whose wholly-owned corporations are
beneficiaries of the trust, it is appropriate to "pierce" through the trust entity for purposes
of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act and attribute income received from the
Neptune Trust as income received from Mr. Kamphausen.
Thus, we would consider both the Neptune Trust and Mr. Kamphausen as
economic interests of Councilmember Wilson.

Florida Street P roperty:
Councilmember Wilson and his domestic partner each own twenty-five percent of
Wilson Enterprise, LLC, which is held by the Wilson Family Trust. Wilson Enterprise,
LLC owns the Florida Street Properly.
Your facts indicate that Councilmember Wilson has an interest in the Wilson
Family Trust pursuant to terms of Regulation 18234(c). Therefore, the assets of the
Wilson Family Tntst would be considered as assets of Mr. Wilson as an individual.
(Talley Advice Letter, No. 4-96-204; Silver Advice Letter, No. 4-96-066; Irmas Advice
Letter, A-92-199.)
Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson has a real property interest in the Florida
Street Property.
Although you included the Florida Street Property in your facts, you do not seek
advice regarding a specific governmental decision regarding this economic interest of
Councilmember Wilson's, therefore we do not analyze it further. We include it in our
analysis simply because we have identified it as an economic interest that must be taken
into consideration with regard to governmental decisions that may come before the
councilmember.
Personal Finances:
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103; regularion 18703.5.) A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing. (lbid.)
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Wilson's personal
finances as the result of the proposed development agreements and other governmental
decisions. But note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have an effect on Councilmember Wilson's personal finances.
Step 4: Are councilmember wilson's economic interests directry or
indirectly involved in the decisions?
Sources of Income:
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a person, including business entities and sources
of income to a public official, is directly involved in a decision before the official's


Pa-ee No. 12
agency when the source of income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar reqllest or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the subject person."
For purposes of determining the materiality standard to be applied under Step 5,
sollrces of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the
rules in Regulation 18704.1(b) are deemed indirectly involved under Regulation 18705.3
ARC:
The facts you present indicate that ARC would not be initiating, or be the subject
of, or a named party in, any decision before the city council or the Agency that involves
clients of ARC.
Therefore, based on the facts presented, ARC is not directly involved in the
govemment decisions involving development or other projects of its clients:
For indirectly involved persons, including business entities, the materiality
standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) (See Step 5
below for discussion of the applicability of Regulation 18705.1.)
The Neptune Trust antl Mr. Kamohausen:
The facts yoLl present indicate that neither Neptune Trust nor Mr. Kamphallsen
would be initiating, or be the subject of, or a named party in, any decision before the city
council or the Agency." Therefore. they are deemed indirectly involved.
('We note that you stated in your letter that "it is clear that Councilmember Wilson has a conflict
based on the Neptune loan and must disqualify himself tiom deciding on any matter having a material
financial ef'fect on Neptune."
Personal Finances:
It is presumed that Councilmember Wilson has an ecbnomic interest in his
personal finances and those of his immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. If there is any financial effect on an official's
personal finances, they are deemed to be directly involved in a decision. Therefore, if
there is an interest, it is directly affected by the decision.
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Wilson's personal
finances as the result of upcoming decisions, but note that a conflict of interest may arise
if it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on his personal
finances.
For instance if the city council decisions to approve green fees, tournament
member fees, and other fees involving the Vallejo Golf Club, lnc. will impact
Councilmember Wilson's personal finances or those of his immediate family either
positively or negatively (such as increasing or reducing the amount he pays in
membership or tournament fees), his personal finances are deemed to be directly
involved in a decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps 5 & 6: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Wilson's economic interest(s)?
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is first necessary to
identify the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standards will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (ln re Thorner, sllpra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (1n
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
Sources of Income
Business EntiN:
For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of less than
$500,000 for the most recent fiscal year) the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(cXa) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( 1)-(3),
the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $ 20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more;
of,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of g 20,000 or
more."
Thus, if the economic impact of the decisions regarding ARC and its clients or the
Neptune Trust meets the above thresholds, the impact will be material.
According to your facts, governmental decisions where ARC is named the
architect of record by one of its clients will result in ARC's annual gross revenues
increasing or decreasing by $20,000 or more. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that
the materiality standard will be met as the result of those decisions. Accordingly,
Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in making, or influence any decision
in which ARC is named by one of its clients as the architect of record on a project, or on
any project that will have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on ARC.
Sources qf Income Who Are Individuals:
Under Regulation 18705.3, an effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a sollrce of income to an official if any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."
The above materiality standard is applicable to Mr. Kamphausen, who is a source
of income to Councilmember Wilson.
You have not included any information regarding possible financial impacts of
the decisions on Mr. Kamphausen. However, if any decision meets the above thresholds,
the impact will be material. Therefore, Councilmember Wilson may not participate in
those decisions involving Mr. Kamphausen that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect upon Mr. Kamphausen.
Nents Rule
In addition, there is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in
cases where there is a "nexus" between duties an official owes to a source of income and
to the official's own public agency. The materiality threshold is understandably much
lower when a public official is paid to accomplish some action that is within the official's
public decision-making authority. Under the nexus rule, "Any reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed
material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
(Regulation I 8705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official,
we presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in
situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and
control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice
Letter, No. A-03-1471' Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.)
You state in your letter that Councilmember Wilson is the Chief Financial Officer
and Vice President of ARC. A nexus may exist, and a material financial effect may
occLlr, if a particular governmental decision in which a high-level administrator like
Councilmember Wilson is asked to participate would also achieve, defeat, aid or hinder a
policy, plrrpose, or goal of ARC. (see Moser, supra; Ronkin Advice Letter, No. A-94-
310.)
You have not presented enough facts for us to analyze fully whether the nexus test
is met. Councilmember Wilson must consider whether the various govemmental
decisions before the city cor.rncil will achieve a goal or purpose that he is paid to bring
about. If a nexus is found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on ARC from a
governmental decision relating to various development projects, even a penny's worth, is
deemed material.
Personal Financial Effects :
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in regulation
18705.5, which provides in pertinent part:
"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public _official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period."7 lRegulation 18705.5)
Thus, if the economic impact of the decisions on Councilmember Wilson's
personal finances meets the above threshold, the impact will be material.
You have not provided any information regarding potential effects of these
decisions on councilmember wilson's finances. Therefore, we cannot make a
determination regarding this issue. However, if the decisions will increase or decrease
Councilmember Wilson's personal expenses (such as Club membership fees or other
Club related expenses), income, assets, or liabilities or that of his or her immediate family
by $250 in any 12-month period, this materiality standard will be met as the result of
these decisions.
steps 7 & 8: Public Generally and Legally Required participation
Exceptions
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 1 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called Llpon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict
of interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies
only in certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be
paralyzed from acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
' Under regulation 18705.5. when determining whether a governmental decision has a material
flnancial efl'ect on a public otTicial's economic interest in his or her personal t'inances. neither a tlnancial
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the oflicial. nor a flnancial etfbct on the
gross revenues, expenses. or value ofassets and liabilities ofa business entity in which the otlicial has a
direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered.
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Cor-rncilmember Wilson's
circumstances, so we will not address them further.

QUESTION: 1. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to development
projects of the following Architectural Resources Collaborative, Inc. ("ARC") clients:
Triad Downtown Vallejo, LLC; Empress Theatre Associates, LLC; Triad 236 Georgra
Street, LLC;Triad Communities, LP, Triad Development, Inc.?
2. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to Lennar Mare
lsland, LLC's ("Lennar") development of the Mare Island upland parcel ("EETP") or
other Lennar development projects?
3. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to Touro
University's ("Touro") development of the Campus Parcel and Reuse Area 1A?
4. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions related to the approval of
fees, capital improvement projects or the annual budget and budget amendments for the
East Course and West Course?
5. May Councilmember Wilson participate in decisions involving Mr. Buck
Kamphausen?

CONCLUSION: 1-3. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in governmental decisions that
come before the city council or the city's redevelopment agency involving ARC clients if
there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on ARC, which is a source of
income to him. Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in
making, or influence governmental decisions where ARC is named the architect of record
on these clients' projects, or in other decisions that result in ARC's annual gross revenues
increasing or decreasing by $20,000 or more.
Additionally, Councilmember Wilson may not make, participate in making, or
influence any decision in which the nents rule applies (as discussed below). Specifically,
he must consider whether governmental decisions before the city council or
redevelopment agency will achieve a goal or purpose, which will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on ARC or any source of income to him, which he is paid to
bring about. If a nexus is found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on ARC,
even a penny's worth, is deemed material.
4. Councilmember Wilson may not participate in these decisions if there is a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his personal finances. Specifically, he
may not participate if any of these decisions result in his personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities (or those of his immediate family) increasing or decreasing by at least
$250 in a 12-month period.
5. Mr. Kamphausen, through his control of the Neptune Trust, is a source of
income to Councilmember Wilson. Therefore, Councilmember Wilson may not
participate in decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon Mr. Kamphausen. Specifically, Councilmember Wilson may not participate in
decisions that affect Mr. Kamphallsen's income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or more.
